output: Implemented the review-pass fixes for the M1c timeline-store migration and captured verification evidence. This pass tightens the mounted availability boundary and initial store bootstrap instead of touching unrelated timeline behavior.

The branch/base prerequisite remains unresolved on this checkout: `git branch --show-current` is still `megaplan/m1a-settings-store`, and `src/shared/state/selectionStore.ts` is still only present as an untracked worktree file rather than committed base history. I did not try to rewrite branch topology from this pass.

Manual browser-only gate items remain unverified in this shell session: the required 10-minute drag/resize/selection/playback/AgentChat/pending-add manual validation still needs a human run before accepting the milestone.

No additional row-isolation code change was needed in this pass. The existing `TrackListRenderer` split from the prior pass was revalidated by rerunning `TrackListRenderer.test.tsx` and `TimelineCanvas.test.tsx`, so I did not reopen that hot-path implementation.

task_updates.executor_notes: Rechecked the execution preconditions. The corrected milestone paths still exist in the worktree, but the branch/base prerequisite is still not satisfied here: the checkout is not on the requested M1c branch off `main`, and `selectionStore.ts` still is not committed on the execution base. I did not attempt to paper over that topology issue with new git surgery from this pass.

Revalidated the provider-owned add-to-editor contract by adding mounted-vs-unmounted store tests around `useAddToVideoEditor`. The new coverage proves the nullable-safe behavior still distinguishes `editor not mounted` from `mounted timeline store`, which was the load-bearing external contract called out in review.

The migration surface and hot-path risk inventory from the earlier pass still held. This pass specifically revalidated the external nullable-safe caller path and the timeline hot path by rerunning provider and timeline regression tests after the bootstrap fix.

`timelineStore.ts` itself did not need another code change, but I added direct regression coverage that exercises its mounted availability boundary through `TimelineStoreProvider` plus `useAddToVideoEditor`. The new tests prove an unmounted provider still returns the staged flow, while a mounted store takes the immediate-drop path.

Fixed the remaining bootstrap risk in `useTimelineState.ts` by seeding the external timeline store during the initial render only when the store is still unmounted, then keeping the existing layout-effect sync for later updates. That removes the first-render placeholder-window for mounted descendants without turning every render into a store write.

No imperative sub-hook control flow was flattened or widened in this pass. The bootstrap change stays in `useTimelineState.ts`, and the regression runs confirmed the existing drag/resize/persistence hook files still behave under the broader video-editor suite.

Added direct regression coverage for the removed-context replacement boundary. The new tests prove that callers outside a mounted editor still stage pending adds, while mounted timeline-store consumers still register the asset and drop it immediately, preserving the pre-migration safe-hook semantics.

The row-isolation fix from the prior pass remained intact. I reran the targeted `TrackListRenderer` and `TimelineCanvas` suites after the bootstrap fix to make sure the new store seeding did not reopen the non-dragged-row rerender path.

Captured the reviewer-facing evidence in this pass through explicit regression coverage and the bootstrap comment in `useTimelineState.ts`. The new tests make the mounted-boundary decision concrete and the bootstrap change is narrowly documented where the same-render seeding happens.

Verification was rerun for this pass: the throwaway repro script showed the unmounted store still stages (`mounted: false`, `phase: staged`) while the mounted store drops immediately (`mounted: true`, `handleAssetDrop` called once); `npx tsc --noEmit` passed; targeted timeline/provider tests passed; the broader `src/tools/video-editor` suite passed (`55` files, `348` tests); and the full `npm test` suite exited 0. I am still not marking the milestone accepted because the required manual browser validation cannot be performed from this shell-only session.

sense_check_acknowledgments.executor_note: Rechecked the checkout state and corrected-path files before editing. The path corrections still hold in the worktree, but the committed-M1b base prerequisite is still not actually satisfied on this checkout.
Revalidated the provider-pathway contract by adding mounted-vs-unmounted `useAddToVideoEditor` coverage. The external staged-add behavior remains distinct from the mounted immediate-drop path.
The earlier grep-backed inventory remained sufficient; this pass focused on the specific external safe-hook caller and bootstrap gap that review called out.
`timelineStore.ts` still provides the mounted availability boundary; the new provider test coverage now exercises that boundary directly through `TimelineStoreProvider` and `useAddToVideoEditor`.
Updated `useTimelineState.ts` so the store gets its first real slices before descendants render, then keeps the post-commit layout-effect sync for later updates.
No sub-hook state machines were moved into Zustand in this pass. The only behavioral change was the initial store bootstrap timing in `useTimelineState.ts`.
The new add-to-editor tests verify that migrated consumers still preserve the mounted-vs-staged boundary after the legacy contexts were removed.
No new row-isolation edit was required here; the prior `TrackListRenderer` split was rerun against the targeted timeline suites and still holds.
The reviewer-facing evidence is now more concrete: there is explicit test coverage for the mounted boundary and an inline note where the initial bootstrap happens in `useTimelineState.ts`.

Automated verification was rerun end to end for this pass, including a throwaway repro script, `npx tsc --noEmit`, targeted timeline tests, the broader video-editor suite, and the full `npm test` suite. Manual browser validation is still outstanding and blocks acceptance.
